title: Working with Remotes
content:
### Adding a Remote

A remote is a URL to another copy of the repo:

```bash
git remote add origin https://github.com/user/repo.git
git remote -v                      # List remotes
```

### Pushing to a Remote

```bash
git push origin main               # Push main to origin
git push -u origin feature         # Push + set upstream tracking
git push --all                     # Push all branches
git push origin --delete feature   # Delete remote branch
```

The `-u` flag sets the upstream, so future `git push` / `git pull` works without arguments.

### Pulling from a Remote

`git pull` is `git fetch` + `git merge`:

```bash
git pull                           # Fetch + merge upstream changes
git pull origin main               # Pull specific branch
git pull --rebase                  # Fetch + rebase instead of merge
```

### git fetch vs git pull

| `git fetch` | `git pull` |
|---|---|
| Downloads objects/refs only | Downloads + integrates |
| Does not update working tree | Updates working tree |
| Safe to inspect changes first | Directly merges changes |

### Remote Tracking Branches

Track branches like `origin/main` show where remotes stand:

```bash
git branch -r                      # List remote branches
git remote show origin             # Detailed remote info
```

### Multiple Remotes

You can have several remotes (e.g., origin + upstream):

```bash
git remote add upstream https://github.com/other/repo.git
git fetch upstream
```
codeExample:
git remote add origin https://github.com/user/my-project.git
git push -u origin main
# Later:
git pull
git fetch origin
git log --oneline origin/main

quiz.question: What is the difference between git fetch and git pull?
options: fetch downloads without merging, pull merges automatically
pull downloads without merging, fetch merges
